---
title: "Module 06: Metrics, Imbalance, and SHAP"
---

## Learning objectives

- Pick **metrics** that match prevalence and error costs (not accuracy by default).
- Read **confusion matrices**, **ROC**, and **PR** curves on imbalanced data.
- Use **VIP** and **SHAP** as **communication** tools — not causal proof.

[← Module library](index.qmd) · [Module 07 — Metric tuning lab](module-07-penguins-choose-metrics.qmd) · [Day 4 Part E](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-e-metrics)

---

## On the slides (Day 4)

| Part | Topic | Slide link |
|------|--------|------------|
| E (first) | Rare Gentoo, upsampling honesty | [Imbalance block](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-e-metrics) |
| E (metrics) | Toolbox, confusion matrix, ROC/PR | [Part E](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-e-metrics) |
| F | Random forest VIP | [Part F — VIP](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-f-vip) |

Tuesday lecture tunes on **accuracy only**; Thursday Part E introduces the full toolbox.

---

## Metrics toolbox

| Metric | Question it answers | When to use |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| **accuracy** | Overall correct? | Balanced classes, symmetric costs |
| **sensitivity** (recall) | Of true positives, how many caught? | Screening — missing a case is costly |
| **specificity** | Of true negatives, how many caught? | Avoid false alarms |
| **precision** | Of predicted positives, how many correct? | Confirmation after a screen |
| **F1** | Balance precision & recall | Single summary when both matter |
| **ROC-AUC** | Ranking across thresholds | Separability; can look fine when positives are rare |
| **PR-AUC** | Precision–recall area | **Rare positive** class |

In code, use **`yardstick::spec`** for specificity so R does not confuse it with a **`spec`** model object.

---

## Why accuracy misleads

On **imbalanced** data (many Adelie, few Gentoo in the teaching slice), a model that **always predicts the majority class** can show **high accuracy** while missing **every** minority case — useless for screening.

Numeric example: if prevalence is 90% negative and 10% positive, an always-negative model gets 90% accuracy but 0% sensitivity for the positive class.

The slides compute a **majority-class baseline** before comparing upsampling ([Part E](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-e-metrics)).

---

## Class imbalance and resampling

- **`step_upsample()`** duplicates minority rows **in training folds** — helps when the learner **ignores** the rare class.  
- On **separable** data (bill space already splits species), upsampling may **not** improve CV metrics — duplicating easy points adds noise.  
- Always report **sensitivity** and **PR-AUC** for rare events, not accuracy alone.
- Fold-level intuition: a training fold with 72 Adelie / 8 Gentoo becomes roughly balanced after upsampling, while the assessment fold remains untouched for honest scoring.

Lab: [Day 4 Block D — Imbalance](../exercises/mlss2026-lab-exercises.qmd) (microbiome `Late`).

---

## Confusion matrix

Rows = **true** class; columns = **predicted** class.

- **False negatives** (true Gentoo, predicted Adelie) — costly in screening.  
- **False positives** — costly if treatment is invasive.

Practice reading the **minority row** on the [Day 4 slides](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-e-metrics).

```{r}
#| label: m06-conf-demo
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| message: false
library(yardstick)
# After augment(fit, test_data):
# conf_mat(truth = y, estimate = .pred_class)
```

---

## ROC vs PR

- **ROC:** sensitivity vs \(1 - \text{specificity}\) across thresholds — good when both classes matter.  
- **PR:** precision vs recall — often more informative when **positives are rare**.

Threshold example: at threshold 0.2 you might get recall 0.90 but precision 0.18; ROC can still look decent while PR reveals large false-alert burden.

---

## Variable importance (VIP)

- **`vip`** on tree ensembles: how often splits use each predictor (impurity).  
- **Correlated** predictors **share** importance — not proof that changing one mm of bill **causes** species shift.  
- Full chapter: [Day 4 Part F](../slides/day-04-thursday.html#/part-f-vip).

---

## SHAP (pointer)

- **SHAP** attributes a **single prediction** to features relative to a baseline.  
- **TreeSHAP** for forests/boosting; **KernelSHAP** for other engines.  
- **Guardrail:** association \(\neq\) intervention. See [Module 06 limits](../curriculum-outline.qmd) and course humility prompts.
- Local-example framing: for one prediction, SHAP might attribute +0.30 log-odds to bill length, +0.15 to flipper size, and -0.10 to island relative to baseline.

---

## Course links

- **Slides:** [Day 4 (Thursday)](../slides/day-04-thursday.qmd)
- **Metric choice in tuning:** [Module 07](module-07-penguins-choose-metrics.qmd)
- **Notebook:** [penguins-species-multiclass.Rmd](../notebooks/penguins-species-multiclass.Rmd)
- **Curriculum:** [outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd)
